coredata.dc:title: H2O2-assisted detection of melamine using fluorescent probe based on corn cob carbon dots-Ionic Liquid-Silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Yuxin
Hao, Jiawei
Arkin, Kamile
Bei, Yuyang
Ma, Xuesong
Shang, Qingkun
Che, Weilong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new sustainable melamine detection method was developed. Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) were successfully prepared by ionic liquid (1-Allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, IL) hydrothermal method using agricultural waste corn cob as the carbon source, and combined with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to construct fluorescent probe CDs@IL-AgNPs. The probe was characterized and the formation mechanism of the probe was discussed. The direct detection of H2O2 and the indirect sensitive detection of melamine were realized. The detection limit of melamine was 0.94 μmol/L, which is far lower than the minimum allowable amount of melamine in milk powder (7.95 μmol/L). The high sensitivity and selectivity probe was used to detect melamine in commercial dairy products, and the recovery rate of standard addition was between 94 % and 110 %. This study provides valuable new application ideas for the detection of melamine in dairy products and the low-carbon and environmentally friendly treatment of agricultural waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melamine
Detection
Corn cob
Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of H2O2 and Mel by CDs@IL-AgNPs
Detection of Mel in dairy products
H2O2-assisted detection of Mel by CDs@IL-AgNPs probe
Detection of Mel in dairy products

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22172021 , 21872022 , 21573039 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs@IL (the upper right inset is the HRTEM image, the upper left inset is the particle size distribution of CDs@IL); (B) XRD pattern, (C) XPS survey, (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum (inset is a photo of CDs@IL under UV light and sunlight), (E) PL spectra and (F) excitation–emission matrix of CDs@IL.

(A) PL emission spectra of CDs@IL and AgNPs in different volume ratios; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@IL, AgNPs, and CDs@IL-AgNPs; (C) Zeta potential of CDs@IL and AgNPs (error bars represent standard deviations (n = 5)); (D) PL spectra of CDs@IL and UV–vis absorption spectrum of AgNPs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) PL emission spectra of H2O2 mixed with CDs@IL-AgNPs; (C) PL emission spectra of CDs@IL-AgNPs with different concentration of H2O2; (D) the linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and H2O2 concentration (error bars represent standard deviations (n = 3)).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) PL emission spectra of CDs@IL-AgNPs added to solutions of Mel reacted with H2O2, where (H2O2 + Mel) represents the solution after the reaction of H2O2 and Mel; (C) PL emission spectra of CDs@IL-AgNPs added to solutions of 1–20 μmol/L of Mel reacted with H2O2; (D) the linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and Mel concentration (error bars represent standard deviations (n = 3)). (Note: The concentration of hydrogen peroxide was always controlled to 16.67 μmol/L.)
Comparison of different nanomaterial for the detection of Mel.
The determination of Mel in milk and milk powder.

head.ce:title: H2O2-assisted detection of melamine using fluorescent probe based on corn cob carbon dots-Ionic Liquid-Silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new sustainable melamine detection method was developed. Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) were successfully prepared by ionic liquid (1-Allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, IL) hydrothermal method using agricultural waste corn cob as the carbon source, and combined with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to construct fluorescent probe CDs@IL-AgNPs. The probe was characterized and the formation mechanism of the probe was discussed. The direct detection of H2O2 and the indirect sensitive detection of melamine were realized. The detection limit of melamine was 0.94 μmol/L, which is far lower than the minimum allowable amount of melamine in milk powder (7.95 μmol/L). The high sensitivity and selectivity probe was used to detect melamine in commercial dairy products, and the recovery rate of standard addition was between 94 % and 110 %. This study provides valuable new application ideas for the detection of melamine in dairy products and the low-carbon and environmentally friendly treatment of agricultural waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Melamine (Mel), an important chemical raw material, is widely used in the synthesis of Mel resin, which can be used in the plastics industry and textile industry (Li et al., 2017). Due to its special chemical structure rich in nitrogen (mass fraction of N is 66.7 %), it is often illegally added to dairy products to falsely increase the protein content. Mel can hardly be metabolized by the human body, and it is easy to adsorb oxalic acid, tannic acid, calcium and other substances in the urinary system to form stones (Liang et al., 2020). Long-term consumption of dairy products containing Mel can easily lead to malnutrition, kidney stones, kidney failure and even death (Hau, Kwan, & Li, 2009). Therefore, it is important to develop a method for the determination of Mel in dairy products. High-precision detection methods have been developed, including high performance liquid chromatography (Filazi, Sireli, Ekici, Can, & Karagoz, 2012), mass spectrometry (Yang et al., 2009), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Goscinny, Hanot, Halbardier, Michelet, & Van Loco, 2011), etc. However, the above-mentioned methods require expensive testing instruments, complicated testing procedures, and long testing times (Liang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a quick and easy detection method.

As an important high-yield food crop in the world, corn is very important to the food industry and aquaculture. But with that comes the massive waste of corn cob, which makes it one of the most abundant agricultural residues in the world (Fatmawati et al., 2021). Currently, the main method of disposal of corn cob is to burn it as fuel, which can also cause serious air pollution. How to develop and utilize corn cob to improve its economic value has always been a problem that many scholars have been trying to explore and solve.

In recent years, the fabrication of nanomaterial-based sensors has increasingly become a research hotspot (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022). Nanotechnology offers unlimited potential for the development of food compound sensors. For example, there have been reports on the application of nanomaterials in food pesticide residue sensing (Buledi et al., 2022) and food dye sensing (Bijad, Karimi-Maleh, Farsi, & Shahidi, 2018). As a new type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have undergone extensive development since their first discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), and have shown promising applications in analytical detection (Tang et al., 2021), biomedicine (Yi et al., 2021), catalytic reactions (Majumdar, Mandani, Bhattacharya, Sarma, & Sarma, 2017), and optoelectronic devices (Molaei, 2019). CDs have many excellent properties, such as simple synthetic method, wide selection of precursors, resistance to photobleaching, good biocompatibility, and easy derivatization (Liu, Chen, Li, & Huang, 2019). The above properties have become the starting point for the continuous development of CDs. In theory, all carbon-containing substances can be used as carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs. The types of precursors can be divided into two categories, one is organic pure substances, such as citric acid (Qu et al., 2013), phenylenediamine (Jiang et al., 2015), dopamine (Lu et al., 2017), rhodamine B (Ma, Sun, Pan, Yu, & Wang, 2020). The fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of CDs synthesized by light-utilizing organic reagents is high, but the price is expensive and the synthesis process is prone to pollution. Another precursor, biomass, is developed from this. Biomass carbon sources are natural products, and as precursors of CDs, they have the advantages of being eco-friendly, cheap, readily available, green, and widely available (Meng et al., 2019). Researchers have found that many natural products can be selected as precursors, such as black pepper (Tang et al., 2021), carrots (D’souza, Chettiar, Koduru, & Kailasa, 2018), magnolia (Wang, Shi, et al., 2020), milk (Wang & Zhou, 2014). Biomass-derived CDs have been greatly developed in recent years, and their properties are comparable to those of CDs with organic reagents as precursors. For example, Wang et al. (2020) synthesized CDs using passionflower as a precursor for label-free detection of Ag+ ions with a detection limit as low as 1.2 nmol/L. They also tuned the emission wavelength of CDs by controlling the reaction temperature and time, resulting in blue, green and yellow CDs (Wang, Chen, et al., 2020). Li et al. (2022) successfully synthesized small CDs with reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging activity from Salvia miltiorrhiza. The CDs also have Ca2+ mobilization properties and can act as both Ca2+ signal amplifiers and ROS scavengers in crop improvement applications (Li et al., 2022). Liu and his colleagues reported high-performance deep red-emitting carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) made from yew leaves with an emission wavelength of 676 nm and a QY as high as 59 % (Liu, Geng, et al., 2020). However, most of the reported precursors for the preparation of biomass-derived CDs, although natural, have other uses, such as food or medicine. It is very important to convert low-value biomass waste into useful materials, and to realize real waste-to-treasure. Therefore, agricultural waste corn cob was selected as the precursor to synthesize CDs in this work. The main component of corn cob is cellulose, which is basically insoluble in water. Some studies have shown that ionic liquids containing chlorides are effective solvents for cellulose. They can dissolve cellulose through hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl functional groups and solvent anions (Swatloski, Spear, Holbrey, & Rogers, 2002). There has been report of CDs obtained by microwave method by mixing ionic liquids with bio-based precursors (Pham-Truong, Petenzi, Ranjan, Randriamahazaka, & Ghilane, 2018).

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have good physicochemical properties and can be combined with CDs to form composites for chemical sensing (Zaib, Sarfaraz, Akhtar, & Shahzadi, 2021). In this study, the agricultural waste corn cob was used as the precursor for the first time to obtain CDs with stable optical properties under the aid of ionic liquid (IL). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs@IL reached 15.15 %. The fluorescent probe CDs@IL-AgNPs was constructed by utilizing the quenching effect of AgNPs on CDs@IL. H2O2 could oxidize AgNPs and destroyed the quenching effect of AgNPs, and then restored the fluorescence of CDs@IL. Based on this, the linear relationship of CDs@IL-AgNPs detection of H2O2 was established. Subsequently, by means of the redox reaction between H2O2 and Mel, the detection of Mel by CDs@IL-AgNPs was realized with a detection limit of 0.94 μmol/L. Converting corn cob with low economic value into a fluorescent probe capable of detecting H2O2 and Mel not only provides a new solution for the reuse of agricultural waste, but also provides a new material for the rapid detection of Mel.

Corn cobs were obtained from a farmer friend. Non-formula milk powder and milk were purchased from the supermarket. Other chemical reagents, such as ionic liquid (1-Allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, IL), silver nitrate, sodium citrate, ascorbic acid, H2O2, Mel, amino acids and various food additives (Methionine (met), Tryptophan (trp), Valine (val), Lysine (lys), Threonine (thr), Phenylalanine (phe), Leucine (leu), Sucralose (TGS), Sodium cyclamate (SC), Aspartame (APM), Superoxide dismutase (SOD), Sodium benzoate (NaA), Potassium sorbate (E202), Vitamin C (VC)) were purchased from Macklin Company (China), and they are all analytical grade samples. The ultrapure water used in the experiment was filtered through Millipore ultrapure water system (USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320, China). Ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Cary 7000, USA) was used to analyze the characteristic absorption of different materials. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained by a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 PLUS, Japan). Particle size distribution chart and Zeta potential data were collected on Microtrac Nanotrac Wave Ⅱ (United States). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was recorded using Thermo ESCALAB 250 (United States) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out by Nicolet 6700 (United States) Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed with a Smartlab (Japan) X-ray powder diffractometer. Time-resolved PL decay data was obtained on Edinburgh Fluorescence Spectrometer (FLS920P, UK) full-featured fluorescence spectroscopy system.

0.02 g of corn cob flour was ultrasonically dispersed in an ionic liquid aqueous solution consisting of 0.30 g of ionic liquid and 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave liner and heated at 180 °C for 9 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, a yellow carbon dot solution was obtained, denoted as CDs@IL. The dark brown carbon dot solution obtained without ionic liquid under the same conditions is denoted as CDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured by a reference method. The reference material was quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4. The formula for calculating the QY of CDs@IL is as follows:
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ S
η X
/
η S
2
(
A S
/
A X
)
(
F X
/
F S
)
where ϕ X represents the QY of CDs@IL, ϕS represents the QY of quinine sulfate (ϕS = 54 %), and ηX and ηS represent the refractive index of the solvent in which CDs@IL and quinine sulfate are located, respectively. AX and AS represent the absorbance of CDs@IL and quinine sulfate solution, respectively, FX, FS are the integrated area of fluorescence spectra of CDs@IL and quinine sulfate solution, respectively.

First, nano-silver was synthesized using ascorbic acid and sodium citrate as reducing agents and stabilizers (Hussain, Al-Thabaiti, & Khan, 2014). 2.08 mg of ascorbic acid and 16.67 mg of sodium citrate were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and the pH was adjusted to 11 with 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution. The solution was placed in an ice bath and 62.5 μL (30 mmol/L) AgNO3 solution was added dropwise with stirring. After 30 min of reaction, the solution was transferred to a boiling water bath. 0.25 mL (30 mmol/L) AgNO3 solution was added dropwise to it, and the reaction was continued for 60 min to obtain a yellow-green nano-silver solution (AgNPs). Then, CDs@IL (245 mg/L) and AgNPs were mixed at a volume ratio of 1:20 and shaken for 10 s, and the solution was standed for 3 min to obtain CDs@IL-AgNPs nano fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of H2O2 and Mel by CDs@IL-AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the response of CDs@IL-AgNPs nano fluorescent probes to H2O2 and the reaction between H2O2 and Mel, the detection of Mel can be realized. The detection was carried out in three steps: (1) 200 μL of H2O2 solutions were added to 1 mL CDs@IL-AgNPs nano fluorescent probes, and the mixture was allowed to stand at 35 °C for 40 min. The fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 425 nm was measured and recorded. (2) 200 μL of Mel solution was added into 200 μL H2O2 solution and the mixture was left at 35 °C for 40 min. (3) 1 mL CDs@IL-AgNPs was added to the mixed solution of (2) and allowed to stand for 40 min. The fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 425 nm was measured and recorded. By changing the concentration of H2O2 or Mel, the content of Mel can be determined under optimal conditions. Three parallel experiments were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Mel in dairy products
ce:section.ce:para: Dairy products needed to be pretreated before the determination. Specifically, 5.0 g of dairy products were dissolved in 25 mL of ultrapure water, and then a mixed solution of trichloroacetic acid and acetonitrile was added to remove protein and fat in the dairy products. The obtained mixed solution was uniformly mixed by ultrasonic treatment, and then centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane and the Mel content was determined according to the above method. A Mel standard was quantitatively added to the supernatant to determine the recovery of the method.

A series of characterization methods were used to explore the morphological, structural and optical properties of CDs@IL. Fig. 1 A is the TEM image of CDs@IL. It can be seen that CDs@IL has a uniform size distribution with a particle size of 1.5 ± 0.5 nm, and also has a clear crystal lattice. The lattice spacing was about 0.21 nm, which coincided with the (100) plane of graphene (Lu et al., 2017; Ding, Wang, Tang, & Qiu, 2021). The XRD pattern of CDs@IL is shown in Fig. 1B. CDs@IL had a broad absorption peak between 20° and 50°, which can be inferred that CDs@IL was an amorphous carbon material. Fig. 1C shows the XPS spectrum of CDs@IL. It is clear that CDs@IL was mainly composed of four elements: C, O, N, and Cl. To further determine the bonding situation of these four elements, their high-resolution XPS spectra were separately processed and shown in Fig. S1. The C 1 s band of the CDs@IL can be fitted with two binding energy peaks at 284.6 and 285.9 eV, which correspond to the CC/CC and CN/CO bonds respectively. As shown in O 1 s spectrum, the signal peak of 532.4 eV is attributed to the C-OH/COC bond. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum reveals the existence of CN (401.4 eV) bond. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cl 2p can be deconvoluted into two peaks located at 196.98 eV and 198.47 eV, corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Cl atom respectively.

The FTIR spectrum of CDs, IL and CDs@IL were shown in Fig. S2. The absorption peaks of IL at 3400, 3030 and 1580 cm−1 correspond to -NH/-OH, unsaturated CH and CC stretching vibration, respectively. The absorption peak at 1200 cm−1 is due to the influence of Cl. Unlike IL, the FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs@IL are similar, both containing -OH/-NH (3300 cm−1), saturated CH bonds (2950 cm−1), CO (1750 cm−1) and R-OH (1020 cm−1). According to literature reports, the ionic liquid whose anion is Cl− has a strong dissolving function for cellulose (Swatloski et al., 2002). It is speculated that the role of IL is to increase the solubility of cellulose in water. Comparing the FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs@IL, it can be found that although the peak shapes are similar, the absorption peak of CDs@IL is shifted to a higher wavenumber direction to that of CDs. The possible reason is that the hydrogen bonds of cellulose are broken by Cl− during the formation of CDs@IL.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (shown in Fig. 1D) indicated that CDs@IL had absorption at 275 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition. Combining with the properties of the raw material structure, it can be concluded that CDs@IL has a certain unsaturated structure, including unsaturated groups derived from CC in lignin and abundant heteroatoms in raw materials.

Fig. 1E shows the PL spectra of CDs, IL and CDs@IL. IL did not have fluorescence emission performance, but with its assistance, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL was 8 times higher than that of CDs. The QY (using quinine sulfate as a reference substance) were 15.15 % and 1.89 % for CDs@IL and CDs, respectively. This further proved that IL can help cellulose dissolving in water. Besides, CDs@IL had dual-wavelength excitation and dual-wavelength emission. The two maximum excitation wavelengths were 375 and 395 nm. The maximum emission wavelengths were 405 and 425 nm. The excitation–emission matrix of CDs@IL is shown in Fig. 1F. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 330 nm to 420 nm, the position of the maximum emission peak was fixed at 405 nm and 425 nm, illustrating CDs@IL had no excitation-dependence. This may be resulted from its uniform particle size distribution. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@IL was calculated to be 12.51 ns (χ2 = 0.9540) by the fluorescence decay curve (Fig. S3).

The effect of external conditions on the fluorescence emission intensity was investigated (shown in Fig. S4). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL remained basically unchanged in the acidic to neutral environment, while it decreased in the alkaline environment (shown in Fig. S4A). The fluorescence of CDs@IL was almost completely quenched at pH 14. The pH dependence of CDs@IL may be related to the abundant hydroxyl and amino groups in its structure. Under different pH conditions, these hydroxyl and amino groups undergo protonation and deprotonation reactions, resulting in CDs@IL exhibiting different fluorescence intensities. Fig. S4B shows the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL. Since the thermal motion of CDs@IL accelerated with increasing temperature, a small fraction of CDs@IL aggregated and the fluorescence intensity decreased. When the temperature increased from 0 °C to 100 °C, its fluorescence intensity decreased by 10 %. The fluorescence of CDs@IL hardly changed after 30 days of storage at 4 °C (refrigerator) (as shown in Fig. S4C), indicating its good temporal stability. The intensity of CDs@IL only decreased by 10.3 % after continuous irradiation under UV lamp for 1 h, which suggested its good resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S4D). In addition, the effects of some common cations, anions and amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL were also tested. As shown in Fig. S5 , the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL was hardly affected by other ions except Fe3+ and Fe2+. The above test results can show that CDs@IL has good stability.

The TEM and UV–vis spectrum of AgNPs are shown in Fig. S6. The particle size of AgNPs was about 20 nm. AgNPs had an absorption peak at 425 nm, which was completely consistent with the properties of silver nanoparticles reported in previous literature. The above characterization results demonstrated the successful synthesis of AgNPs. Fig. 2 A shows the proportional relationship between CDs@IL and AgNPs when constructing nanofluorescent probes. With the increase of AgNPs volume, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL gradually decreased. When the volume ratio of the two was 1:20 (CDs@IL:AgNPs), the fluorescence of CDs@IL was almost completely quenched, thus this ratio was chosen to construct the nanofluorescent probe. In Fig. 2B-D, the interaction between CDs@IL and AgNPs was analyzed to explain the quenching process between them. There are various forms of quenching after CDs interact with the target, such as static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), inner filter effect (IFE) (Su & Yang, 2021). From the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2B), at the same concentration, the absorbances of CDs@IL-AgNPs nano-fluorescent probes (such as at 275 nm and 425 nm) were smaller than those of AgNPs. It was also smaller than the linear sum of the absorbances of CDs@IL and AgNPs, which indicated that there was a certain interaction between CDs@IL and AgNPs. AgNPs can release Ag+ in solution (Chernousova & Epple, 2013). A very small amount of Ag+ may exist in the AgNPs solution, which can form complexes with the abundant amino groups on the surface of CDs@IL. In turn, the balance of Ag/Ag+ in the colloidal solution is shifted, so the absorption intensity derived from AgNPs decreases. This proves that the quenching of CDs@IL by AgNPs follows the SQE mechanism. The zeta potential values of CDs@IL and AgNPs were determined to be + 3.5 and −1.6 mV (as shown in Fig. 2C). This indicated that the surfaces of CDs@IL and AgNPs were oppositely charged. Electrostatic interactions may cause slight aggregation and lead to fluorescence quenching, i.e. SQE. Since the UV–vis spectra of AgNPs and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@IL overlapped to a large extent (as shown in Fig. 2D), this suggested the possible existence of an IFE or DQE mechanism. According to the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@IL decreased from 12.51 ns to 11.19 ns after adding AgNPs (Fig. S7), we found that the fluorescence quenching of CDs@IL by AgNPs did not originate from the IFE mechanism. Usually, the DQE process can be represented by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of the quencher (AgNPs), respectively, [Q] is the equilibrium concentration of the quencher, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (L·mol−1). When taking F0/F as the ordinate and the equilibrium concentration of AgNPs as the abscissa, we obtained a straight line (as shown in Fig. S8) with a slope (KSV) of 15,155 L·mol−1, which was in line with the Stern-Volmer equation, namely DQE. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of AgNPs on CDs@IL includes SQE caused by the complex formation between CDs@IL and Ag+, and DQE caused by the interaction between AgNPs and excited CDs@IL.

H2O2 is a strong oxidizing substance, which can oxidize Ag to Ag+. Will it change the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL-AgNPs? The effect of H2O2 on the absorption spectra of the CDs@IL-AgNPs probe was first investigated, as shown in Fig. 3 A. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@IL before and after adding H2O2 did not change significantly, indicating that H2O2 did not interact with CDs@IL. After adding H2O2 to CDs@IL-AgNPs, the characteristic absorption peak at 425 nm belonging to AgNPs completely disappeared, which was because AgNPs was oxidized to Ag+. Similarly, in the fluorescence spectrum of Fig. 3B, it can be seen that H2O2 had no effect on the fluorescence spectrum of CDs@IL, but greatly increased the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL-AgNPs. This is well understood. It was AgNPs that cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs@IL. When the AgNPs were oxidized to Ag+ by H2O2, this quenching effect naturally disappears.

Can the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL be fully recovered if the amount of H2O2 is sufficient to completely oxidize the AgNPs? As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence of CDs@IL-AgNPs gradually recovered with the increase of H2O2 concentration. This phenomenon existed when the final concentration of H2O2 was in the range of 1.67 to 16.67 μmol/L. When the final concentration of H2O2 exceeded 16.67 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity no longer increased. After the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence of the system could only recover to 77 % of that of CDs@IL, which could not be completely recovered. It may be that H2O2 can only destroy the dynamic quenching between CDs@IL and AgNPs, but not the static quenching, so the fluorescence intensity cannot be fully recovered. Based on the effect of H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL-AgNPs, a linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 of the probe and the concentration of H2O2 was established (as shown in Fig. 3D, where I is the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL-AgNPs with different concentrations of H2O2, and I0 is the fluorescence intensity of the blank control CDs@IL-AgNPs). The linear equation was obtained from three parallel experiments with a standard deviation of less than 0.195 for each data point. The linear equation is y = 0.9912x + 0.364 (R2 = 0.9955).

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2-assisted detection of Mel by CDs@IL-AgNPs probe
ce:section.ce:para: If a substance can react with H2O2, it may weaken the oxidation effect of H2O2 on AgNPs, so that the fluorescence intensity of the probe may be changed. Mel is a substance with strong electron donating ability, which is easy to react with H2O2 (Song et al., 2020). The possibility of detecting Mel with CDs@IL-AgNPs fluorescent probe assisted by H2O2 was explored. First, it was investigated whether Mel could directly interact with the probe. From Fig. S9A-B, Mel had no effect on the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs@IL or CDs@IL-AgNPs, indicating that neither CDs@IL nor CDs@IL-AgNPs respond directly to Mel. Second, it was studied whether the probe CDs@IL-AgNPs responded to Mel in the presence of H2O2. Since H2O2 can interact with both AgNPs and Mel, there was a competitive relationship between AgNPs and Mel, so it was important to control the reaction sequence. Two comparative experiments were designed. One was that the CDs@IL-AgNPs were first reacted with H2O2 and then Mel was added, and the results are shown in Fig. S9C-D. It was found that the absorption and emission spectra of the H2O2-containing system did not change after the addition of Mel, which indicated that the reaction of AgNPs with H2O2 would not be reversed by the addition of Mel. The second was to mix H2O2 and Mel first and then add CDs@IL-AgNPs, the results are shown in Fig. 4 A-B. It was found that with the increase of Mel concentration, the characteristic absorption peak derived from AgNPs gradually increased, and correspondingly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@IL-AgNPs gradually decreased. The presence of Mel made the effect of H2O2 on the absorption and emission spectra of CDs@IL-AgNPs disappear. As shown in Fig. 4C-D, when the concentration of Mel increased from 1 to 20 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of the probe CDs@IL-AgNPs gradually decreased. There is a linear relationship between I/I 0 and Mel concentration (where I 0 is the fluorescence intensity of blank control CDs@IL-AgNPs), and the linear equation is y = -0.6841x + 16.8653 (R2 = 0.9973). The linear equation was obtained from three parallel experiments with a standard deviation of less than 0.207 for each data point. The lowest limit of detection (LOD) of CDs@IL-AgNPs for Mel was 0.94 μmol/L, which was lower than the limit of quantification of Mel in dairy products (6.34 μmol/L) by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) in the national recommended standard (GB/T 22388-2008). It is worth mentioning that the national standard stipulates that the content of Mel in non-formulated dairy products cannot exceed 7.925 μmol/L (2.5 mg/kg). The LOD of the method developed in this work for Mel is well below this value. Compared with the Mel detection method reported in the literature (Table 1 ), the CDs@IL-AgNPs nano-fluorescent probe had a wider detection range and lower detection limit.

Selectivity is one of the important indicators to evaluate the performance of fluorescent probes. The responses of essential amino acids, food additives, and inorganic ions (all at a concentration of 20 μmol/L) commonly found in dairy products to the probe were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S10. It is shown that except potassium sorbate (E202) and vitamin C (VC), which can weaken the fluorescence intensity of the probe, other substances have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the probe. This was because both E202 and VC can be oxidized by H2O2, thus interfering with the determination of Mel. To explore the competitive responsiveness of CDs@IL-AgNPs to Mel, these non-responsive species were separately mixed with Mel at 20 times the concentration of Mel, and then mixed with hydrogen peroxide. CDs@IL-AgNPs were added to the above mixture, and the fluorescence intensity of the probe was similar to that of pure Mel solution (as shown in Fig. S11). All data in Figs. S10 and S11 are listed in Table S1. It can be seen that the CDs@IL-AgNPs nano-fluorescent probe has certain selectivity and anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Mel in dairy products
ce:section.ce:para: Pure milk and milk powder (non-formulated dairy products) were purchased from supermarkets, and a mixed solution of trichloroacetic acid and acetonitrile was added to remove proteins and fats. The standard addition method was used to quantitatively analyze Mel in dairy products and compared with the national standard method (HPLC method), and the results are listed in Table 2 . No Mel was detected in the actual samples. After adding different standard substances, the recovery rate was in the range of 94 % to 110 %, and the standard deviation of the five determination results was less than 5 %. Importantly, the detection results using the fluorescent probe CDs@IL-AgNPs were similar to those obtained by the standard HPLC method. It is worth noting that when the amount of melamine added is 5 μmol·L−1, it cannot be detected by the standard HPLC method because the detection limit is only 6.34 μmol·L−1. However, such a low melamine content was accurately detected using the probe CDs@IL-AgNPs. Therefore, the CDs@IL-AgNPs nano-fluorescent probe can be used for the detection of Mel in actual dairy products.

The development and utilization of waste biomass materials is one of the important contents of China's sustainable development strategy. The search for a sensitive detection method for Mel illegally added to dairy products has always been one of the research hotspots in analytical chemistry. In this work, carbon dots of CDs@IL were prepared by using waste corn cob as raw material and ionic liquid as solvent, and further composed of fluorescent probe CDs@IL-AgNPs with Ag nanoparticles. The probe can not only directly detect the content of H2O2, but also detect melamine with the aid of H2O2, with a detection limit as low as 0.94 μmol/L, and the detection results of actual dairy products can meet the requirements of the national standard method. The research work in this paper not only provides a new idea for the development of biological waste, but also provides a new sensitive method for the detection of melamine.

Yuxin Zheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jiawei Hao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Kamile Arkin: Methodology, Validation, Data curation. Yuyang Bei: Investigation, Data curation. Xuesong Ma: Visualization. Qingkun Shang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Weilong Che: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172021, 21872022, 21573039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134415.

ce:section.ce:para:
